quiz_metadata.title: One-Way ANOVA Post-Test
quiz_metadata.description: Answer the following questions based on your current understanding. Answer to the best of your ability.

shared_stimulus.text: An organizational psychologist is studying whether three different onboarding programs affect new employee productivity scores (measured on a 100-point scale) after 90 days. She randomly assigns 60 new hires to one of three programs — Standard Onboarding (n = 20), Mentorship-Based Onboarding (n = 20), and Self-Directed Onboarding (n = 20). Results: Standard M = 71.4 (SD = 6.2), Mentorship M = 80.1 (SD = 6.8), Self-Directed M = 68.9 (SD = 12.5). A One-Way ANOVA produces F(2, 57) = 9.84, p = 0.0002.

questions.title: Between-Group Variability — Definition
questions.question_text: In this study, between-group variability refers to:

answers.text: The spread of productivity scores among employees within the Standard Onboarding group
How much the three group means (71.4, 80.1, 68.9) differ from the overall mean
The total variability in productivity scores across all 60 employees
The difference between the largest and smallest individual scores in the dataset

questions.title: Within-Group Variability — Definition
questions.question_text: Which of the following is the best example of within-group variability in this study?

answers.text: The gap between the Mentorship mean (80.1) and the Self-Directed mean (68.9)
The difference between the highest and lowest group averages
The variability in productivity scores among the 20 employees in the Self-Directed group
The F-statistic of 9.84

questions.title: Interpreting the F-Statistic Value
questions.question_text: The F-statistic of 9.84 tells us that:

answers.text: Between-group variability is about 9.84 times larger than within-group variability
Within-group variability is about 9.84 times larger than between-group variability
There are 9.84 units of difference between each pair of group means
The p-value is 9.84 times smaller than 0.10

questions.title: Unequal Variance Concern
questions.question_text: The Self-Directed group has SD = 12.5, while the other two groups have SD = 6.2 and 6.8. What concern does this raise?

answers.text: The group means are too similar for ANOVA to be meaningful
The Self-Directed group may be violating the equal variance assumption
The sample sizes are too small to compute an F-statistic
The between-group variability is artificially inflated

questions.title: Effect of Reducing Within-Group Variability
questions.question_text: Suppose the same group means (71.4, 80.1, 68.9) were observed but all three groups now had SD = 2.0. What would happen to the F-statistic?

answers.text: It would decrease, because the group means are more compressed
It would stay the same, because the means did not change
It would increase, because within-group variability decreased
It would become negative, because the standard deviations are now equal

questions.title: Effect of Reducing Between-Group Variability
questions.question_text: Now suppose the group means were much closer together — Standard = 72.1, Mentorship = 73.0, Self-Directed = 71.8 — with standard deviations unchanged. What would happen to the F-statistic?

answers.text: It would increase, because there are still three groups
It would decrease, because between-group variability is now smaller
It would stay the same, because within-group variability did not change
It would double, because the number of comparisons stays fixed at 3

questions.title: Signal vs. Noise
questions.question_text: In the context of One-Way ANOVA, within-group variability is best described as:

answers.text: The "signal" — it represents meaningful differences caused by the treatment
The "noise" — it represents background variation that makes it harder to detect real differences
The denominator of the p-value calculation
A measure of how different the group sample sizes are from each other
The within-group degrees of freedom, based on sample sizes within each group
The between-group degrees of freedom, equal to the number of groups minus one
The total number of groups in the study
The number of pairwise comparisons being made

questions.title: Conclusion from p-value
questions.question_text: The ANOVA produces p = 0.0002. At a significance level of 0.10, what is the correct conclusion?

answers.text: Fail to reject H₀; there is no evidence that any group means differ
Reject H₀; there is significant evidence that at least one group mean differs from the others
Reject H₀; all three group means are significantly different from each other
Accept H₀; the between-group variability is too small to matter

questions.title: Conditions for a Large F-Statistic
questions.question_text: Which of the following conditions would tend to produce a large F-statistic? Select all that apply.

answers.text: Group means are spread far apart from each other
Within-group variability is small relative to between-group differences
All group means are approximately equal to the overall mean
The standard deviations within groups are very large
Sample sizes are large enough to reduce within-group error estimates

questions.grading_note: Full credit for selecting A, B, and E only. Partial credit at professor's discretion.

questions.title: Interpreting a Non-Significant Result
questions.question_text: A different researcher gets F(2, 57) = 0.61, p = 0.55. What is the most accurate interpretation?

answers.text: The group means are proven to be identical
The between-group variability is large relative to within-group variability
The within-group variability is large enough that the observed mean differences are not statistically convincing
There is a mistake in the analysis because F cannot be less than 1

questions.title: Total Variability Decomposition
questions.question_text: In a One-Way ANOVA, total variability in the data is decomposed into:

answers.text: Variability due to sampling error and variability due to measurement error
Between-group variability and within-group variability
Variability in the means and variability in the standard deviations
Explained variability and unexplained variability in a regression sense only

questions.title: Impact of High SD in One Group
questions.question_text: The Self-Directed group's large SD (12.5) compared to the other groups primarily affects the ANOVA by:

answers.text: Inflating between-group variability, making the F-statistic appear larger than it should
Inflating within-group variability, which reduces the F-statistic and makes it harder to detect real group differences
Having no effect because only means are used in the F-statistic calculation
Causing the Mentorship group mean to appear artificially high by comparison

questions.title: What a Large F-Statistic Tells Us
questions.question_text: Which of the following statements are true when a One-Way ANOVA produces a large F-statistic? Select all that apply.

answers.text: Between-group variability is large relative to within-group variability
The observed differences among group means are unlikely to be due to chance alone
All individual group means are proven to be significantly different from each other
The null hypothesis should be rejected if the p-value is below the significance level
Within-group variability is larger than between-group variability

questions.grading_note: Full credit for selecting A, B, and D only. Partial credit at professor's discretion.

questions.title: Synthesis — Interpreting the Full Result
questions.question_text: Based on F(2, 57) = 9.84 and p = 0.0002, which of the following is the most complete and accurate conclusion?

answers.text: We reject H₀; all three onboarding programs are proven to differ from each other, and no further testing is needed since the F-statistic already identifies which groups differ

We reject H₀; the large F-statistic indicates between-group variability greatly exceeds within-group variability, suggesting at least one onboarding program produces meaningfully different productivity outcomes, and a post-hoc test should be used to identify which programs differ
We fail to reject H₀; although the F-statistic is large, the p-value of 0.0002 is too extreme to be considered reliable evidence under standard testing conditions

We reject H₀; the result confirms that Mentorship Onboarding causes higher productivity than the other two programs, since it has the highest group mean and the between-group variability exceeds within-group variability
